title: rtdl — Research on Tabular Deep Learning (reference library: FT-Transformer, MLP/ResNet, numerical embeddings PLE/Periodic/PLR)
authors: Yury Gorishniy, Ivan Rubachev, Artem Babenko, et al. (Yandex Research)
venue: GitHub (yandex-research/rtdl, Apache-2.0); sub-packages rtdl_revisiting_models (Apache-2.0) and rtdl_num_embeddings (MIT, v0.0.12, Apr 2025). Packages the code for 'Revisiting Deep Learning Models for Tabular Data' (NeurIPS 2021) and 'On Embeddings for Numerical Features in Tabular Deep Learning' (NeurIPS 2022).
tokenization_method: Reference feature-tokenizer stack. (A) Base tokenizer (FT-Transformer 'FeatureTokenizer'): every feature becomes ONE d-dimensional token. Numerical token = per-feature affine T_j = b_j + x_j * W_j with a distinct learned weight vector W_j in R^d and bias b_j in R^d per feature (a Linear(1,d) applied independently to each scalar). Categorical token = per-feature embedding-table lookup plus a per-feature bias. A learned [CLS] token is appended; the transformer attends over (1 + n_features) tokens and the CLS output is the prediction head input. (B) Numerical-embedding upgrade (rtdl_num_embeddings): the scalar->vector step is REPLACED/preceded by richer per-feature modules — LinearEmbeddings (the affine above), LinearReLUEmbeddings, PeriodicEmbeddings (concat[sin,cos] of learnable-frequency projections), PiecewiseLinearEncoding (PLE, a continuous ordinal one-hot over bins), PiecewiseLinearEmbeddings, and the composite PLR = Periodic->Linear->ReLU. These modules emit the per-feature vector that the backbone (MLP/ResNet/FTTransformer) consumes.
feature_types_handled: numerical (continuous) and categorical — passed to the model as two separate tensors (x_cont, x_cat). No first-class temporal/time-series tokenizer, no multicategorical/text/image stype, no learned-missingness signal (caller imputes/encodes NaN before the tokenizer). This is the numerical+categorical core only, narrower than pytorch-frame's multimodal stype coverage.
token_granularity: per-feature: exactly one d-dimensional token per input feature, plus one learned [CLS] token for the transformer backbone. Sequence length = n_features + 1. For MLP/ResNet backbones the per-feature embeddings are flattened/summed rather than attended over.
numerical_strategy: Pluggable per-feature, parameters never shared across features. Choices: raw affine LinearEmbeddings (b_j + x_j*W_j); LinearReLUEmbeddings; PeriodicEmbeddings (sin/cos of x times learnable frequencies c ~ N(0,sigma)); PiecewiseLinearEncoding/Embeddings (PLE: bins via compute_bins, the active bin holds a linear ramp in [0,1], lower bins=1); and the recommended composite PLR (Periodic->Linear->ReLU). compute_bins supports quantile bins (unsupervised) and target-aware tree bins (pass y + tree kwargs, the PLEt variant).
categorical_strategy: Standard per-feature learned embedding table (cat_cardinalities list -> one nn.Embedding region per feature) with a per-feature bias term; index space sized by each feature's cardinality. No target/CatBoost encoding, no hashing, no compositional codes built in.
high_cardinality_handling: None specific. Each categorical feature gets a full embedding table whose size grows linearly with cardinality (same blowup as entity embeddings / FT-Transformer). The library offers no hashing, quotient-remainder, or compositional compression; high-cardinality string IDs must be pre-encoded by the caller (e.g. hashed/QR) before being handed to cat_cardinalities.
temporal_handling: none — i.i.d. static tabular assumption. No patching, recurrence, or channel-as-token. A time-varying half of a 70+-feature set would need an external temporal tokenizer; rtdl only handles the static numerical+categorical columns.
scales_to_many_features: Tokenizer stage is O(n_features) and embarrassingly parallel (per-feature independent), so producing tokens for 70+ columns is cheap. The dominant cost is the FTTransformer backbone, whose attention is O(n_features^2) over the per-feature token sequence; the library's ONLY native scaling lever is linformer_kv_compression_ratio (a Linformer low-rank kv compression option in FTTransformer) which makes attention sub-quadratic in sequence length. Per-feature non-shared embeddings also multiply parameter count by feature count (documented ~10x-1000x blowup in the num-embeddings paper). No published 70+-feature scaling benchmark in the library itself; scaling claims are architectural, with Linformer the one knob to test.
downstream_model: MLP, ResNet, FTTransformer (transformer). The embedding modules are backbone-agnostic — the 2022 paper's headline is that MLP-PLR (MLP + PLR embeddings) rivals or beats FT-Transformer while being cheaper, so the transformer is not mandatory.
pretraining_objective: none — supervised end-to-end. Target-aware PLE bins (compute_bins with y) are a one-time preprocessing fit on the train split, not a pretraining objective. No SSL, no cross-table transfer.
key_results: As a library it reproduces the two source papers. From those: FT-Transformer is a strong universal DL baseline competitive with GBDTs (NeurIPS 2021); MLP-PLR reaches average rank ~3.0 vs vanilla MLP ~8.5, beats XGBoost and rivals CatBoost on 11 mid/large datasets (NeurIPS 2022) — first DL parity with GBDT on California-Housing/Adult. The library's contribution is the reference, reproducible implementation rather than a new number.
baselines_compared: Through its source papers: CatBoost, XGBoost, vanilla MLP/ResNet/Transformer, AutoDis (CTR numerical embedding), thermometer/binary binning. As a library it is itself the de-facto baseline implementation that downstream tabular-DL papers (TabR, TabM, ExcelFormer, pytorch-frame) compare against and import.
transferable_idea: Borrow the per-feature tokenizer pattern directly: (numerical scalar -> richer per-feature embedding module) + (categorical -> per-feature embedding table) -> stack into one [B, n_features, d] token sequence + [CLS]. For the 70+-feature numerical half, default to PLR (or quantile-PLE when you want interpretability/cheaper compute), and reach for the built-in Linformer kv-compression to keep attention tractable as n_features grows. The rtdl_num_embeddings modules are drop-in PyTorch and the cleanest runnable starting point for the continuous half of a heterogeneous tokenizer.
limitations: (1) Numerical+categorical only; no temporal, multicategorical, text, or learned-missingness support — narrower than pytorch-frame for a truly heterogeneous 70+-feature set. (2) Same embedding functional form applied to every numerical feature (no per-feature distribution adaptation). (3) High-cardinality categoricals are unaddressed — embedding-table blowup is the caller's problem. (4) Per-feature non-shared embeddings cause large parameter blowup and O(n_features^2) attention; Linformer compression is the only mitigation and is not benchmarked at 70+ features in-library. (5) Original monolithic rtdl package is deprecated; functionality is split across rtdl_revisiting_models + rtdl_num_embeddings, so 'rtdl' API examples online may be stale.
pitfalls: 1) 'rtdl' the PyPI package is DEPRECATED — the old rtdl.FTTransformer / rtdl.FeatureTokenizer import path no longer reflects the maintained code; use rtdl_revisiting_models (Apache-2.0) and rtdl_num_embeddings (MIT). Two different licenses across the split. 2) The library packages prior-work tokenizers; it is engineering infrastructure, not a new tokenization advance — cite it as the reference implementation of FT-Transformer / PLR, not as a method. 3) Target-aware PLE bins (compute_bins(..., y=...)) are fit on labels — refit on the full set or per-fold leakage creates target leakage (the proposal's named anti-pattern). 4) The Periodic/PLR gain is DL-specific and does NOT transfer to GBDTs, so it is not model-agnostic feature engineering. 5) FTTransformer expects x_cont and x_cat passed SEPARATELY and CLS/embedding params should be excluded from weight decay via make_parameter_groups() — easy to get wrong and silently hurt results. 6) No scaling benchmark inside the library at 70+ features; the Linformer knob is present but its accuracy cost on wide heterogeneous tables is untested here.
relevance_to_topic: The canonical, maintained reference implementation of the two tokenizers this topic centers on (FT-Transformer per-feature affine token + PLE/Periodic/PLR numerical embeddings); the most direct runnable scaffold for the numerical+categorical core of a 70+-feature tokenizer, and the home of the one built-in scaling lever (Linformer kv-compression).
code_available: yes — hub https://github.com/yandex-research/rtdl (Apache-2.0); rtdl_revisiting_models (pip install rtdl_revisiting_models, Apache-2.0, MLP/ResNet/FTTransformer + Linformer option); rtdl_num_embeddings (pip install rtdl_num_embeddings, MIT, v0.0.12 Apr 2025, LinearEmbeddings/PeriodicEmbeddings/PiecewiseLinear*/PLR + compute_bins). Actively maintained by Yandex Research.
